content:
The libraries that quietly turned repetitive scripts into tools I actually use every week.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Anna Khromova on Unsplashread here.
For the first couple of years of writing Python, I had a habit that probably sounds familiar.

👉 Enroll Now & Start Learning
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Whenever I wanted to automate something, I immediately started writing functions.
Then more functions.
Then utility files.
Then helper classes.
A few hours later, I had hundreds of lines of code solving a problem that modern AI libraries can now solve in twenty.
That realization completely changed how I build automation projects.
The biggest lesson wasn’t learning another framework. It was learning to stop asking:
“How do I code this?”
and instead ask,
“Which library already solves 80% of this problem?”
That single mindset shift has probably saved me hundreds of hours over the past year.
If you’re building automation with Python, these are the five libraries I reach for first.
1. OpenAI — The Automation Multiplier
Most developers think of large language models as chatbots.
I think of them as automation engines.
Need to rename thousands of files?
Generate reports?
Rewrite emails?
Extract structured data from invoices?
Categorize customer feedback?
Those aren’t “AI problems.”
They’re automation problems that happen to involve language.
One of the first automations I built replaced hours of manual document cleanup. Instead of writing dozens of regex rules, I passed the messy text to an LLM with a carefully designed prompt.
The result wasn’t just shorter code.
It was code that handled edge cases I hadn’t even considered.
A simple workflow usually looks like this:

• Collect data

• Build a prompt

• Send it to the model

• Receive structured output

• Continue the automation
That’s it.
The interesting work isn’t calling the API.
It’s designing reliable prompts and validating the responses.
Pro Tip: The quality of your automation is usually limited more by your prompt than your Python code.
2. Pandas — Because Automation Usually Means Data
I used to underestimate Pandas.
I thought it was only useful for data science.
I couldn’t have been more wrong.
Almost every automation project eventually becomes a data problem.
CSV files.
Excel sheets.
Logs.
Reports.
API responses.
Databases.
Pandas makes all of them feel like they’re using the same interface.
One automation script I wrote processed thousands of records every morning.
Before Pandas, the code was filled with nested loops, temporary dictionaries, and enough indexing to give me a headache.
Replacing that logic with a few DataFrame operations reduced both the code size and the number of bugs.
Cleaner code isn’t just easier to read.
It’s easier to trust.
3. Playwright — My Favorite Way to Automate the Web
Browser automation has existed for years.
But Playwright made it enjoyable.
If you’ve ever copied data between websites manually, downloaded reports every morning, or clicked through the same dashboard repeatedly, you already have an automation opportunity.
Playwright handles tasks like:

• Logging into websites

• Filling forms

• Downloading reports

• Clicking dynamic elements

• Taking screenshots

• Testing web applications
One project automated a workflow that previously required opening six browser tabs every day.
Watching that script complete the entire process in under a minute felt oddly satisfying.
Almost like hiring an intern who never gets tired.
4. Pydantic — The Library That Saved Me From Bad Data
Automation breaks for one simple reason.
Data rarely looks the way you expect.
Someone changes an API.
A field disappears.
A number becomes text.
A required value suddenly becomes null.
Without validation, these bugs often appear halfway through a long-running script.
Pydantic catches many of them immediately.
Instead of wondering why your automation failed twenty minutes later, you find out before the workflow even begins.
That kind of early feedback becomes invaluable as projects grow.
I now use it in almost every API-based automation.
Not because I enjoy writing models.
Because I enjoy debugging less.
5. Rich — The Library That Makes Automation Feel Professional
This one surprised me the most.
Most automation scripts print messages like this:
Processing...
Done...
Error...
Retrying...Technically, that’s enough.
Practically, it’s painful.
Rich transforms terminal applications with colorful logs, progress bars, tables, syntax highlighting, tracebacks, and status indicators.
The first time I added proper progress bars to a long-running automation project, I realized something interesting.
The script hadn’t become faster.
It simply felt faster because I could see what was happening.
Good user experience isn’t only for web applications.
Command-line tools deserve it too.
The Pattern I Didn’t Notice At First
Looking back, I realized something.
None of these libraries actually automate anything by themselves.
They each solve one specific bottleneck.

• OpenAI understands language.

• Pandas organizes information.

• Playwright controls browsers.

• Pydantic validates data.

• Rich improves visibility.
When you combine them, you stop writing isolated scripts.
You start building complete automation systems.
That’s where Python becomes incredibly fun.
What I’d Recommend If You’re Just Starting
Don’t try to learn fifty AI libraries.
Learn five really well.
Build one project.
Then build another.
Every automation project teaches something that the documentation never will.
The best Python developers I know aren’t the ones who memorize APIs.
They’re the ones who recognize repetitive work and immediately think,
“I could automate that.”
As Bill Gates famously said:
“Automation applied to an efficient operation will magnify the efficiency.”
Python has never had a better ecosystem for making that happen.
The challenge isn’t finding powerful libraries anymore.
It’s noticing the problems around you that are worth solving.
And once you start seeing them, it’s surprisingly difficult to stop.

Thank you for being a part of the community
